title: 17 — The Anointing of the Sick
html:
Big Question

Does suffering have meaning, or do we just have to endure it?

It was 1574. A 24-year-old Venetian soldier had lost everything: he had gambled away his money, his weapons, even his shirt. With nothing left, sick and abandoned, he found work at a hospital for the incurable in Rome —not out of vocation, but necessity: they gave him room and board. His name was Camillus de Lellis. His temper was violent, his tongue, that of a tavern. The hospital administrators fired him twice. The third time, Camillus was different.

One day, while washing a terminally ill patient, something broke inside him —or rather, something caught fire. He looked at the man before him and saw Christ. «In the sick I see Christ, and in Christ I see the sick,» he would later repeat. Camillus converted, was ordained a priest, and founded the Order of the Ministers of the Sick —the Camillians— the first to wear a red cross on their chests with the motto: «More heart in those hands, brothers.»

St. Camillus de Lellis did not invent the Anointing of the Sick —Christ did— but he rehabilitated it in the popular imagination. For centuries, this sacrament had been wrongly called «Extreme Unction» and understood as the last rite before dying: the announcement of the end. But the Second Vatican Council recovered its original meaning: it is not a sacrament for the dying; it is a sacrament for the sick, for those who suffer, for those weakened by age. It is Christ the physician who touches, who anoints, who heals —not always the body, but always the soul.

The Catechism of the Catholic Church dedicates an entire section to explaining this sacrament (CCC 1499-1532). And it begins with a truth we struggle to accept: illness and suffering are not a punishment; they are a mystery. A mystery that God does not avoid from the outside but accompanies from within.

Activity

Imagine you are sick and cannot go to school or play with your friends. You feel lonely and sad. But one day someone special comes —not just any doctor, but Jesus himself. He places his hands on you, anoints you with blessed oil, and says: «Do not be afraid, I am with you.» That is the Anointing of the Sick: Jesus coming to visit you in your weakness, through the priest.

Draw a cross and around it write the names of people you know who are sick. Then pray a Hail Mary for each of them.

1. Jesus and the sick: the heart of his mission

If you read the Gospels carefully, something leaps off the page: Jesus spent an enormous amount of time with the sick. Not as paperwork, not as a PR campaign. The sick were at the center of his mission. «He healed them all» (Mt 8:16). «He went through all the cities and villages... healing every disease and every sickness» (Mt 9:35).

But we must understand something crucial: Jesus does not heal to prove he is God. He heals because this is who God is. His healings are not tricks; they are signs. They point to something deeper: the total salvation of the human being, body and soul. The Catechism says it this way: «Christ the physician, in healing the sick, shows that he has come to heal the whole person, soul and body. They are signs of the spiritual healing he comes to bring: the forgiveness of sins» (cf. CCC 1503).

Jesus is not scandalized by human suffering. He draws near. He touches lepers —unthinkable in his culture. He lets the woman with the hemorrhage touch his cloak. He stops for blind Bartimaeus when everyone wants him to be quiet. Each healing encounter is personal, concrete, intimate. This is who God is: he does not give generic prescriptions; he looks you in the eye.

Not just curing: accompanying

And here is a point we easily miss: Jesus did not heal all the sick in Israel. If he was God, why not eliminate illness once and for all? Because God's plan is not a plan of efficiency, but of closeness. God does not press a button to fix the world from outside. He enters the world, touches, suffers, dies, and rises. Illness will disappear at the final resurrection; in the meantime, God accompanies it.

This is exactly what the sacrament of the Anointing does. It does not always cure physically —though sometimes it does— but it always heals the soul. It gives strength, peace, and courage to face the illness. It unites the suffering of the sick person to the suffering of Christ. And if it is God's will, it even restores bodily health (CCC 1520).

«When Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic: Son, your sins are forgiven... But so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins —he said to the paralytic—: I tell you, get up, take up your stretcher and go home.» (Mk 2:5,10-11 — cf. CCC 1502)

Notice the order: first forgiveness, then physical healing. The order is not accidental. The true illness is sin; the true health is grace. Physical healing is the outward sign of an inward reality.

Context

Perhaps you come to this class afraid. You know someone who is sick, or you yourself have been in a hospital. The word «anointing» sounds like a farewell. Take a deep breath. The Church has made it very clear that this sacrament is not for the dying. It is for anyone who begins to be in danger from illness or old age (CCC 1511). It can be received several times in one's life. It is not the announcement of death: it is the announcement that Christ will not let you go.

If you have seen this sacrament administered in the past as «the last rite,» do not be frightened: the Church herself corrected that practice. Today we insist that it is a sacrament of life, of healing, of hope.

2. The biblical foundation: James, Mark, and the Good Samaritan

The Anointing of the Sick has one of the clearest biblical foundations of all the sacraments. The Letter of James is so explicit it almost needs no interpretation:

«Is any among you sick? Let him call for the presbyters of the Church, and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord. And the prayer of faith will save the sick man, and the Lord will raise him up; and if he has committed sins, he will be forgiven.»

— Jas 5:14-15

This text contains all the elements of the sacrament: the minister (the presbyters, i.e., the priests), the matter (the oil, the anointing), the form (the prayer of faith), the effects (salvation, relief, forgiveness of sins). The Catechism, following the Council of Trent, sees in these words the institution of the sacrament by the Lord Jesus himself, which the Church practices and teaches (CCC 1510-1511).

But there is more. The Gospel of Mark, the first Gospel to be written, tells us something remarkable about the apostles:

«The Twelve, going out two by two... cast out many demons, and anointed with oil many who were sick and cured them.» (Mk 6:12-13)

From the very first missionary sending, the apostles used oil to heal. This was not magic. It was the visible sign of an invisible grace coming from Christ. The same Church, in its earliest centuries, preserved this practice as a sacred rite: anointing with oil consecrated by the bishop, accompanied by the prayer of faith.

The Good Samaritan: Christ as physician of wounded humanity

There is one image the Fathers of the Church read with particular depth. The parable of the Good Samaritan (Lk 10:25-37). A man is assaulted, beaten, and left half dead by the roadside. A priest and a Levite pass by... and go on their way. But a Samaritan —a foreigner, a heretic in Jewish eyes— stops. «He bandaged his wounds, pouring on oil and wine» (Lk 10:34). He puts him on his own animal, takes him to an inn, and pays with his own money for his care.

St. Augustine and other Fathers saw in this Samaritan Christ himself. Humanity lies wounded by the roadside of history. The ancient religions pass by; they cannot heal. But Christ stops. He bandages our wounds with oil and wine —the sacraments. The oil is the Anointing; the wine, the Eucharist. And he leaves us in the care of the Church, the «inn» where we are tended until he returns.

«The Church has received from the Lord the command to heal the sick and to care for them» (CCC 1509). The sacrament of the Anointing is the Church being faithful to this command: pouring Christ's oil on the wounds of the world.

Connection

Think about the last time you were truly sick. Not a passing cold, but something that laid you flat for days. How did you feel? Fragile, dependent, perhaps frightened. Now imagine that in that moment you had known that the Church has a specific sacrament for you. Not a «farewell rite,» but Christ drawing near to your bedside. That is the Anointing. Do not wait until you are at the threshold of death. If you have a major surgery, a serious illness, or simply the weight of years... ask a priest to anoint you.

St. Teresa of Calcutta used to call a priest for her sick not only at the end, but at the beginning of a serious illness. She knew that the Anointing is not an announcement of death; it is an announcement that Christ fights with you.

3. What does this sacrament do? The effects of the Anointing

The Catechism lists five principal effects of this sacrament (CCC 1520-1523). Do not read them as a shopping list. Take them into your heart. Each one is an immense gift.

First: a particular gift of the Holy Spirit

The first effect is «a particular gift of the Holy Spirit» (CCC 1520). This is not just generic grace. It is a grace specific to the sick person: the grace of consolation, peace, and courage to overcome the difficulties proper to illness. The Holy Spirit does not stay in theory. He touches. He strengthens.

Second: union with the Passion of Christ

This point is perhaps the most disconcerting and the deepest. «The suffering of the sick person, united to the suffering of Christ, is made a means of purification and salvation» (cf. CCC 1521). St. Paul says it with a startling phrase: «I complete in my flesh what is lacking in Christ's afflictions for the sake of his body, which is the Church» (Col 1:24).

What does this mean? That Christ's Passion is insufficient? No. It means that Christ, in his infinite love, associates our sufferings with his own. Not because he needs our pain, but because he wants us to participate in his redemptive work. Our suffering, united to Christ's, has redemptive value. It is not absurd. It is not punishment. It is participation.

The Venerable Fulton Sheen expressed it with an image: human suffering is like a dry sponge. In itself, it has no meaning. But if the sponge absorbs the water of Christ's Passion, it becomes fruitful.

Third: an ecclesial grace

The Anointing is not a private matter between the sick person and God. It is an act of the whole Church. «Those who receive this sacrament contribute, through the grace of the Holy Spirit, to the good of the People of God» (CCC 1522). When a sick person offers their suffering for the Church, for the conversion of sinners, for the missions... they are not wasting time. They are working for the Kingdom. The bed of a sick person can be more fruitful than a theologian's study.

Fourth: preparation for the final journey

The Anointing prepares the sick person for «the passage to the Father's house» (CCC 1523), if God wishes to call them. It is not a sentence; it is a fortress. The grace of the sacrament sustains the sick person at the final hour, overcoming the temptation of discouragement and anguish. We do not die alone: Christ dies with us, and we with him.

Fifth: sometimes, bodily health

Yes. The Catechism says clearly that «if it is conducive to spiritual salvation, the Anointing can also restore bodily health» (CCC 1520). It is not a magic sacrament; it is a sacrament of trust. God knows better than we do what we need. Sometimes physical healing is part of the plan; other times, the grace is something else. But in both cases, the sacrament works.

«Lord, if you are willing, you can make me clean.» Jesus reached out his hand and touched him, saying: «I am willing, be clean.» (Mt 8:2-3)

Going Deeper

The theology of Christian suffering was developed in a particular way by St. John Paul II in his apostolic letter Salvifici doloris (1984). There he explains that human suffering, united to that of Christ, acquires salvific value: it is not useless, but redemptive. Suffering is not a problem God cannot solve; it is a mystery into which God himself enters.

The Church's spiritual tradition also speaks of the «sacrament of the sick» as a consecration: just as baptism consecrates the Christian, the Anointing consecrates the sick person so that their suffering becomes fruitful. It is not a misfortune; it is a vocation.

Recommended reading: Benedict XVI, Spe salvi (2007), especially paragraphs 35-39 on the meaning of suffering in Christian hope. Also St. John of the Cross, who from his own experience of illness and imprisonment wrote: «At the evening of life, you will be examined on love.»

4. Who, when, how? Minister, subject, and celebration

The minister: only a priest or bishop

This is one of the sacraments that only a priest (or a bishop) can administer (CCC 1516). Deacons cannot, lay people cannot. This is not a question of power; it is a question of configuration to Christ the Priest. Only one who has received priestly Ordination can act in persona Christi —in the person of Christ— for this sacrament.

It is beautiful to think of it this way: when the priest anoints the sick person, it is Christ himself who touches. The priest's hands are Christ's hands. The oil he pours is the balm of the Spirit. The prayer he pronounces is the word of God that heals.

The subject: who can receive it?

The Catechism says: «Any member of the faithful who begins to be in danger of death from illness or old age» (CCC 1511). Notice the word: «begins.» It is not necessary to be in agony. It is enough that the illness is serious, or that age takes its toll. An elderly person with ailments can receive it. Someone about to undergo heart surgery can receive it. Someone undergoing cancer treatment can receive it.

And yes: it can be received several times (CCC 1515). If the illness worsens, or if a new serious illness appears, the same faithful person can be anointed again. The sacrament does not run out. Each time you receive it, Christ touches you anew.

Children may also receive it if they have sufficient use of reason to be comforted by the sacrament (CCC 1514). The old practice of not anointing children has been superseded: a child with a serious illness can and should receive Christ's strength.

The matter and the form

The matter of the sacrament is olive oil —or other plant oil— blessed by the bishop (CCC 1512). Each year, at the Chrism Mass on Holy Thursday, the bishop consecrates the oils that will be used by the parishes of his diocese. It is a gesture that unites the whole local Church around its shepherd.

The form is the prayer the priest pronounces while anointing the forehead and hands of the sick person: «Through this holy anointing, may the Lord in his love and mercy help you with the grace of the Holy Spirit. May the Lord who frees you from sin save you and raise you up.» In case of necessity, it is enough to anoint the forehead using a shortened form (CCC 1513).

Activity

Ask in your parish: When was the last Chrism Mass? Who is the bishop of your diocese? The oil of the sacrament you will receive —or have already received— was consecrated by him. Find out: What is his name? How many years has he been a bishop? Pray an Our Father for him.

5. Viaticum and the three sacraments of the end of Christian life

The Church's tradition speaks of three sacraments that prepare the Christian for the encounter with the Lord. They are like three gifts God gives us at the end of the road:

  1. Penance or Reconciliation — the forgiveness of sins, peace of soul.
  2. The Anointing of the Sick — the strength of the Spirit for the final battle.
  3. The Eucharist as Viaticum — the bread for the journey, «food for the way» (CCC 1524).

Notice the order: first the soul is cleansed (Penance), then strengthened (Anointing), and finally nourished for the crossing (Eucharist). This is not accidental. It is divine pedagogy: God does not hurl us into the abyss; he prepares us, strengthens us, feeds us.

Viaticum: the word that says everything

«Viaticum» comes from the Latin viaticum: provision for the journey (via = road). In the ancient world, the viaticum was the money or food given to someone undertaking a long journey. The Church took this word to designate the Eucharist received by the sick person at the end of earthly life: it is the bread that gives strength to cross the frontier of death.

The Catechism says: «The Eucharist, as the sacrament of the Lord's Passover, should be the last sacrament of earthly life. It is the Viaticum, the sacrament of the passage from death to life, from this world to the Father» (CCC 1524).

This is not a minor liturgical detail. It is the culmination of Christian initiation: Baptism made us children, Confirmation made us soldiers, the Eucharist nourished us all our lives... and at the final moment, the Eucharist accompanies us to cross over.

«I am the living bread that came down from heaven. Whoever eats this bread will live forever.» (Jn 6:51)

An important historical clarification

For many centuries, this sacrament was called «Extreme Unction» —the last anointing. The name reflected a practice that, with good intentions, had deformed the sacrament's meaning. People saw it as the announcement of death, and many sick people would not ask for it out of fear. They preferred to die without it rather than «call the priest» and frighten the family.

The Second Vatican Council (1962-1965) recovered the original name: «Anointing of the Sick,» and clarified that «it is not only the sacrament of those who are in the final moment. Certainly, the fitting time for receiving it begins when the faithful begin to be in danger of death from illness or old age» (SC 73, cited in CCC 1511).

This change was not cosmetic. It was pastorally revolutionary: it allowed the faithful to request the Anointing without feeling they were signing their death warrant. Today, the Church encourages the sick to receive this sacrament with faith and hope, not with fear.

Connection

Perhaps you have experienced a situation where no one dared call the priest because «the sick person will be frightened.» This is understandable, but it is a mistake. The Anointing is not the visit of a notary. It is the visit of Christ the physician. As a Christian, you have the responsibility to help the sick members of your family request this sacrament with peace. Do not wait for the last breath. Call the priest when the illness is serious. And prepare the sick person by explaining: «This doesn't mean you are dying right now. It means Christ wants to give you strength for this struggle.»

If your pastor has doubts, remind him charitably what the Catechism says: the Anointing is for those who begin to be in danger, not only for the dying.

6. Connection with Scripture and Tradition

The Anointing of the Sick was not born from some late brainstorm of the Church. It was born from the very heart of the Gospel. Jesus spent his public life healing. And when he sent his followers, he gave them the same power:

«As you go, proclaim that the Kingdom of Heaven is at hand. Heal the sick, raise the dead, cleanse lepers, cast out demons. You received without paying; give without pay.» (Mt 10:7-8)

Mark's Gospel records the first missionary anointing we know of: «The Twelve anointed with oil many who were sick and cured them» (Mk 6:13). And James, the first bishop of Jerusalem, codified the practice for all Christian communities:

«Is any among you sick? Let him call for the presbyters of the Church, and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord. And the prayer of faith will save the sick man, and the Lord will raise him up; and if he has committed sins, he will be forgiven.»

— Jas 5:14-15

Throughout the centuries, the Fathers of the Church bore witness to this practice. Origen (3rd century) mentions penance and anointing as remedies for soul and body. St. John Chrysostom (4th century) calls the oil of anointing «medicine of the soul.» And St. Caesarius of Arles (6th century) exhorted the faithful not to resort to pagan healers, but to the priests of the Church, «who anoint with blessed oil in the name of the Lord.»

Tradition is unanimous: the Anointing of the Sick is a gift of Christ to his Church, received from the apostles and faithfully handed down to this day.

For Reflection

1. What image did you have of the Anointing of the Sick before this lesson? Has anything changed?

Many people grew up with the idea that this sacrament was «the final thing» before dying. If that's your experience, how does it make you feel to know the Church offers it as a sacrament of healing, not just farewell?

2. Do you know anyone who has received the Anointing of the Sick? What was that experience like?

If you don't know anyone, imagine: a relative of yours is sick. Would you dare suggest they ask for this sacrament? What would you say so they wouldn't be afraid?

3. St. Paul writes: «I complete in my flesh what is lacking in Christ's afflictions» (Col 1:24). What does it mean to you that your suffering can have redemptive value?

This isn't about seeking pain or glorifying it. It's about knowing that when suffering comes unbidden, we can unite it to Christ and make it fruitful. Does this thought console you, or do you find it hard to accept?

4. St. Camillus de Lellis was a violent soldier and compulsive gambler before his conversion. What does his story tell you about second chances?

Camillus hit rock bottom before finding God in the sick. Sometimes our own or others' suffering is the place where God waits for us. Have you experienced anything similar?

5. The Eucharist as Viaticum is the «bread for the journey» to the Father's house. How do you prepare for that final journey?

This is not a morbid question. It's the most realistic question there is: we are all going to die. Do you live with the hope that Christ will accompany you? Or would you rather not think about it?

Key Idea

Key Idea: The Anointing of the Sick is not a sacrament for dying. It is a sacrament for living illness with Christ: the one who suffers is not alone. Christ the physician touches, strengthens, and unites their pain to his redemptive Passion. Suffering without Christ is absurd. With Christ, it is fruitful.

Cultural Resources

Art The Disrobing of Christ (El Greco, 1577-1579, Sacristy of Toledo Cathedral). El Greco painted Christ stripped of his garments before the Crucifixion, surrounded by a jeering mob. Christ's serene gaze in the midst of chaos is a visual catechesis: the one who suffers is not defeated; he is at the center of God's plan. Also Christ Healing the Blind by the same painter, where supernatural light emanates from Christ himself.

Saint St. Camillus de Lellis (1550-1614). Patron of the sick, hospitals, and healthcare workers. His story is among the most striking: soldier, gambler, himself sick, fired twice from the hospital... and then founder of an order that revolutionized care of the dying. The Camillians wore a red cross on their chests —centuries before the Red Cross— and their motto was: «More heart in those hands, brothers.» His feast is July 14.

20th Century Saint St. Teresa of Calcutta (1910-1997). Mother Teresa understood as few have that the sick person is not a burden. They are the face of Christ. Her Missionaries of Charity collect the dying from the streets of Calcutta —and from around the world— not to cure them but to give them dignity: to die knowing they are loved. Her words: «Each one of them is Jesus in his most distressing disguise.»

Film Lorenzo's Oil (George Miller, 1992). Not a religious film, but it tells the story of parents fighting against their son's incurable illness. It is a portrait of love that does not surrender to suffering. Also Awakenings (Penny Marshall, 1990), with Robin Williams and Robert De Niro, about neurological patients who temporarily recover life: a powerful metaphor for what grace does when it touches a sleeping soul.

Music Hakuna Group Music — «Huracán» and «Mi Pobre Loca» speak of a God who does not stay in the heights but descends into human clay. Also Pergolesi's «Stabat Mater» (1736), one of the most overwhelming works of sacred music, meditating on Mary's sorrow at the foot of the cross —and in her, the sorrow of every mother at a suffering child.

Literature Fulton J. Sheen, Peace of Soul (1949). The chapter on suffering explains how human pain can be transformed into fruitful prayer. Also C. S. Lewis, The Problem of Pain (1940): a rational defense of suffering in the light of Christian faith, written by a convert who had been a militant atheist. And St. John Paul II, Salvifici doloris (1984), a brief but deeply profound apostolic letter on the Christian meaning of human suffering.

Audiovisual Catechesis Ascension Presents with Fr. Mike Schmitz and Real + True (osvnews.com/real-true) have episodes on the Anointing of the Sick. In Spanish, the Infinito + 1 channel by Juan Manuel Cotelo offers «Catequizis» —short, deep videos on each sacrament. Also Padre Luis Toro on YouTube, with clear catechesis for young people and adults.
